The Quiet Art of Web Scraping

Every page on the web arrives wrapped in noise. Navigation bars, cookie banners, newsletter prompts, and footers crowd around the words a reader actually came for. The craft of extraction is the craft of throwing things away without regret.

Why Extraction Matters

When the linden trees bloom in the courtyard, the gardener does not gather every leaf that falls; she gathers only the blossoms. Good content extraction works the same way. It keeps the argument, the evidence, and the voice of the author, and it lets the chrome fall to the ground where it belongs.

Research workflows amplify the stakes. A language model reading a page pays for every token of boilerplate, so a clean extraction is not cosmetic — it is the difference between an afternoon of work and an afternoon of waste. See this related essay for a longer treatment of the economics involved.

The Practical Rules

First, prefer the main element when the author provides one. Second, distrust anything that repeats across pages of the same site. Third, measure what remains: if the words you kept would not survive being read aloud to a patient friend, you kept the wrong words. These rules are old, boring, and almost always correct.

The tools change every few years, but the discipline does not. Extraction is editing, and editing is judgment. No amount of machinery removes the need to decide what a page is actually about.
